q: When offspring show traits intermediate between two parental phenotypes, this is known as:
e: Incomplete dominance occurs when offspring show traits intermediate between two parental phenotypes.

q: In four o'clocks, crossing red and white-flowered plants produces pink-flowered offspring. What is the biochemical basis for this?
o: The red allele completely masks the white allele.
Both alleles produce an effective product simultaneously.
The level of gene-directed protein production is between that of the two homozygotes.
A homozygous recessive condition blocks the production of a metabolic enzyme.
e: Incomplete dominance has a biochemical basis; the level of gene-directed protein production may be between that of the two homozygotes.

q: Does incomplete dominance support the blending theory of inheritance?
o: Yes, because the offspring have blended phenotypes.
Yes, because the original traits are lost forever.
No, because parental phenotypes reappear in the F2 generation.
No, because it only applies to codominance.
e: Incomplete dominance does not support a blending theory because parental phenotypes reappear in the F2 generation.

q: A pattern of inheritance in which both alleles of a gene are fully expressed is called:
e: Codominance is a pattern of inheritance in which both alleles of a gene are fully expressed.

q: A person with AB blood type has both A and B antigens on their red blood cells. This is an example of:
e: A person with AB blood has both A and B antigens, which is a classic example of codominance where both alleles produce an effective product.

q: What is pleiotropy?
o: Several genes interact to produce a single phenotype.
A single gene exerts an effect on many aspects of an individual's phenotype.
One gene masks the expression of another gene.
Traits are influenced by environmental factors.
e: Pleiotropy occurs when a single gene exerts an effect on many aspects of an individual's phenotype.

q: Marfan syndrome is caused by a mutant gene that is unable to code for the normal production of which protein?
e: In Marfan syndrome, a mutant gene is unable to code for the production of a normal protein, fibrillin.

q: Which of the following is NOT a typical characteristic of individuals with Marfan syndrome?
o: Tall and thin with long legs and arms
e: Individuals with Marfan syndrome tend to be tall and thin with long legs, arms, and fingers; are near-sighted; and have a weak aorta.

q: The absence of an expected phenotype as a result of the masking expression of one gene pair by another is known as:
e: Epistasis is the absence of an expected phenotype as a result of masking expression of one gene pair by the expression of another gene pair.

q: In sweet pea plants, crossing two plants can produce an F2 generation with a 9:7 ratio instead of the expected 9:3:3:1. This is explained by:
o: A homozygous recessive condition blocking a metabolic enzyme
A single gene affecting multiple traits
e: The 9:7 ratio is explained by epistasis, where a homozygous recessive condition blocks the production of a metabolic enzyme.

q: Albino animals inherit an allelic pair (aa) that prevents the production of melanin. This is an example of:
e: Albino animals inherit an allelic pair (aa) that prevents the production of melanin, masking the expression of eye and hair color genes, an example of epistasis.

q: In a system with multiple alleles for a single locus, how many alleles does a single diploid individual inherit?
e: There may be more than two alleles for one locus in a population, but each individual inherits only two alleles.

q: The human ABO blood type system involves how many alleles and how many possible phenotypes?
e: The ABO system involves three alleles, resulting in four possible phenotypes: A, B, AB, and O.

q: The water buttercup (Ranunculus peltatus) has different leaf shapes depending on whether they are above water or submerged. This demonstrates:
e: The aquatic environment influences the phenotype of the water buttercup, demonstrating that both genotype and environment affect phenotype.

q: Primroses produce white flowers above 32 degrees Celsius and red flowers at 24 degrees Celsius. What factor is affecting their phenotype?
e: Temperature can affect the phenotypes of some plants, like primroses, and animals, like Siamese cats.

q: When a trait is controlled by several allelic pairs at different loci having an additive effect, it is known as:
e: Polygenic inheritance occurs when a trait is controlled by several allelic pairs at different loci.

q: In polygenic inheritance, alleles that result in quantitative variations are called:
e: Contributing alleles have an additive effect, resulting in quantitative variations in polygenic traits.

q: Which of the following is an example of a polygenic trait?
e: Examples of polygenic inheritance include seed color in wheat, human skin color, and human height.

q: Because polygenic traits are subject to environmental effects that cause intermediate phenotypes, their frequency distribution forms a:
e: Polygenic traits produce continuous variations whose frequency distribution forms a normal (bell-shaped) curve.

q: Who independently proposed the Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance in 1902?
o: Thomas Hunt Morgan and James Watson
Theodor Boveri and Walter S. Sutton
e: The Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance was independently proposed in 1902 by Theodor Boveri and Walter S. Sutton.

q: Which observation supports the Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance?
o: Chromosomes and alleles of each pair separate during meiosis so gametes have one-half.
Gametes contain diploid numbers of chromosomes.
Genes are completely independent of chromosomes.
e: The theory is supported by the observation that chromosomes and alleles of each pair separate during meiosis so gametes have one-half.

q: Chromosomes that are the same number and kind between sexes are called:
e: Autosomes are nonsex chromosomes that are the same number and kind between sexes.

q: What are the sex chromosomes of a normal human male?
e: The sex chromosomes in the human female are XX; those of the male are XY.

q: In humans, which parent determines the sex of the offspring?
o: The mother, because she provides the X chromosome.
The mother, because of mitochondrial DNA.
The father, because he produces X-containing and Y-containing gametes.
Both parents contribute equally to sex determination.
e: Males produce X-containing and Y-containing gametes; therefore, males determine the sex of the offspring.

q: Any gene located on the X chromosome that does not control a sexual feature is called a(n):
e: An X-linked gene is any gene located on the X chromosome, often missing on the Y chromosome, that does not necessarily control a sexual feature.

q: Which model organism did Thomas Hunt Morgan use to confirm that genes were on chromosomes?
e: Morgan's experiments involved fruit flies, which have an XY sex determination system similar to humans.

q: What was the phenotype of the newly discovered mutant male fruit fly in Morgan's experiments?
e: A newly discovered mutant male fruit fly had white eyes.

q: When Morgan crossed a white-eyed male with a dominant red-eyed female, what was unique about the white-eyed flies in the F2 generation?
o: They had a 1:1 ratio of males to females.
e: The cross yielded the expected 3:1 red-to-white ratio; however, all white-eyed flies were males, supporting an allele for eye color on the X chromosome.

q: Why are human males never considered 'carriers' for X-linked traits?
o: Because they have two X chromosomes.
Because the Y chromosome masks the X chromosome.
Because they only have one X chromosome and express whatever allele is on it.
Because X-linked traits only affect females.
e: Males are never carriers but express the one allele on their single X chromosome.

q: In X-linked inheritance, females who are heterozygous are called:
e: Heterozygous females are carriers; they do not show the trait but can pass it on.

q: Which of the following is an example of an X-linked recessive disorder in humans?
e: One form of color-blindness is an X-linked recessive trait.

q: Because fruit flies have thousands of genes but only four pairs of chromosomes, Sutton concluded that:
o: Each chromosome must hold many genes.
e: Fruit flies have four pairs of chromosomes to hold thousands of genes; Sutton concluded each chromosome must hold many genes.

q: All alleles on a single chromosome form a:
e: All alleles on a chromosome form a linkage group that stays together except when crossing over.

q: Why do linked alleles not obey Mendel's law of independent assortment?
o: Because they tend to go into the gametes together.
Because they are always separated during mitosis.
Because they are located on different chromosomes.
e: Linked alleles do not obey Mendel's laws because they tend to go into the gametes together.

q: What process breaks linkage groups and causes recombinant gametes?
e: Crossing-over causes recombinant gametes and at fertilization, recombinant phenotypes.

q: Mutations are changes in chromosomes or genes. For a mutation to be passed to offspring, it must occur in:
e: Mutations are changes in chromosomes or genes that pass to offspring if they occur in gametes.

q: The condition where an individual has only one of a particular type of chromosome is called:
e: Monosomy occurs when an individual has only one of a particular type of chromosome.

q: The failure of chromosomes to separate properly during cell division is called:
e: Nondisjunction is the failure of chromosomes to separate.

q: Nondisjunction is more common during which phase of cell division?
e: Nondisjunction is the failure of chromosomes to separate; it is more common during meiosis I than meiosis II.

q: A syndrome is defined as:
o: A single gene mutation causing a specific disease.
A distinctive set of physical and mental abnormalities.
The complete loss of a chromosome pair.
A harmless variation in chromosome structure.
e: Survivors of chromosomal number changes are characterized by a distinctive set of physical and mental abnormalities called a syndrome.

q: Turner syndrome is characterized by which chromosomal makeup?
e: Turner syndrome is a monosomy where the individual has a single X chromosome.

q: The most common trisomy among living humans, involving chromosome 21, is known as:
e: Down syndrome is the most common trisomy among humans; it involves chromosome 21.

q: When offspring end up with more than two complete sets of chromosomes (e.g., 3n, 4n), this is called:
e: Polyploidy occurs when offspring end up with more than two complete sets of chromosomes.

q: While polyploidy is rare and often lethal in animals, it is a major evolutionary mechanism in:
e: Polyploidy does not increase variation in animals; it is a major evolutionary mechanism in plants.

q: Environmental factors that can cause chromosomes to break include all of the following EXCEPT:
e: Environmental factors including radiation, chemicals, and viruses, can cause chromosomes to break.

q: A change in chromosomal structure where a segment becomes separated and reinserts at the same place but in reverse is called a(n):
e: Inversions occur when a segment that has become separated from the chromosome is reinserted at the same place but in reverse.

q: When a chromosomal segment is removed from one chromosome and inserted into another nonhomologous chromosome, it is called a(n):
e: Translocations occur when a chromosomal segment is removed from one chromosome and inserted into another, nonhomologous chromosome.

q: Translocation heterozygotes usually suffer from reduced fertility due to:
e: Translocation heterozygotes usually have reduced fertility due to the production of abnormal gametes.

q: Cri du chat syndrome is caused by which type of chromosomal mutation?
e: Cri du chat syndrome is a deletion in which an individual has a small head, is mentally retarded, and has a cat-like cry.

q: What is a potential evolutionary benefit of chromosomal duplications?
o: Multiple copies of genes can mutate differently and provide additional genetic variation.
e: Multiple copies of genes can mutate differently and provide additional genetic variation for a species.
